title: Funeral Homes and Funeral Services

narrative.executive_view: Funeral homes have durable need for licensed, physical and compassionate service, but terminal revenue is pressured by cremation and unbundling rather than by AI. The exact Census scope covers preparing the dead and conducting funerals, including wake facilities, transport and merchandise [S1]; stand-alone cemeteries and crematories sit outside 812210. AI can reduce case administration, communications and obituary drafting, yet most core delivery remains human. The acquisition case is therefore a conventional local-trust and succession roll-up with a modest administrative AI benefit, offset by a long-run shift toward lower-ticket cremation and direct disposition.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI can draft obituaries, organize case data, triage calls, schedule staff, prepare routine correspondence and support pricing or pre-need administration. An NFDA supplier article documents native AI-assisted obituary workflows but also requires human editing and warns about invented details [S12]. Funeral directors still coordinate transport, preparation, ceremonies, merchandise and legal documentation [S5]. Embalming, removals, crematory operation, service direction and family counsel are physical, regulated or relational. The supplied 4-digit role mix includes cemetery grounds labor, so that occupation is treated as sibling-code contamination rather than a funeral-home automation opportunity.
narrative.value_capture: Families pay funeral providers directly and the FTC Funeral Rule requires itemized General Price Lists and consumer choice [S2], so there is no insurer-style payer automatically reclaiming cost savings. Local reputation, embedded pre-need relationships and the need for guidance support retention: even among online planners, nearly 48% still needed a funeral director [S4]. But capture is not complete. Itemization and digital search make prices more comparable, and the 2025 projected cremation rate of 63.4% materially shifts mix toward lower-ticket offerings [S3]. AI savings are likely retained at first, while competitive transparency and mix deflation limit the moat.
narrative.adoption_timing: Digital interaction is already meaningful—nearly 30% of surveyed families completed all arrangements online—yet that is not the same as effective AI labor replacement [S4]. AI obituary tools exist [S12], but the most valuable tasks require licensed review, judgment and compassionate interaction. Current effective adoption is estimated below 50%, with five years to broad use across the addressable administrative task set. Small independent operators, state-specific workflows and reputational risk should keep deployment slower than generic office software.
narrative.consolidation_economics: At least four active consolidators are directly evidenced: SCI acquired 22 funeral locations in 2025 [S6], Park Lawn completed repeated 2025–2026 acquisitions [S8], Carriage acquired eight funeral homes and a cremation business in 2025 [S9], and Foundation Partners has fresh institutional capital and more than 250 locations [S10]. Johnson Consulting states that average-to-larger funeral homes trade around 6–7× EBITDA, with higher values when prized real estate is included [S7]. A 6.5× buy and 7.0× exit use that same range, with the small exit step reserved for a diversified, professionally managed portfolio. Pre-need liabilities, owner labor, real-estate rent and trust earnings must be normalized before applying either multiple.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand survives AI because death still requires disposition, transport, paperwork and often ceremonies, and many consumers value professional guidance [S4]. Revenue quality is nevertheless pressured. NFDA projects cremation at 63.4% in 2025 and 82.3% by 2045, while 2023 median prices were $8,300 for viewing and burial versus $6,280 for a funeral with cremation [S3]. SCI's comparable cremation rate reached 64.4% in 2025 even as average revenue per service rose to $5,823 [S6], showing that pricing can partly offset mix but does not remove the structural risk. Terminal class is therefore pressured, not melting.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The dominant risks are cremation mix, direct-cremation bypass, online price transparency, licensing and ownership rules, and quality-of-earnings treatment of pre-need contracts. SCI's $17.0 billion pre-need backlog illustrates both locked future volume and significant delivery obligations [S6]. The owner-age estimate is cross-industry and the n-band dataset likely understates qualifying targets because its generic margin is below healthy funeral-home margins. The 6–7× multiple source dates from 2022 and includes a real-estate caveat, so current target-specific bids require fresh local comps. These uncertainties justify medium confidence despite strong evidence on category demand and active buyers.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition — 812210 Funeral Homes and Funeral Services
sources.what_it_supports: Defines the exact industry as establishments preparing the dead for burial or interment and conducting funerals, including wake facilities, transport and related merchandise.

sources.title: Funeral Industry Practices Rule
sources.what_it_supports: Summarizes 16 CFR Part 453, including itemized General Price Lists, consumer choice and required disclosures.

sources.title: NFDA Statistics
sources.what_it_supports: Reports projected 2025 cremation and burial rates, the 2045 cremation projection and 2023 median funeral prices for burial and cremation.

sources.title: Americans Embrace Digital Funeral Planning While Still Seeking Professional Guidance
sources.what_it_supports: Reports 2025 consumer-survey findings on online arrangements, continued funeral-director assistance, pre-planning and professional guidance.

sources.title: What Does a Funeral Director Do?
sources.what_it_supports: Describes funeral-director tasks including consultation, transportation, preparation, legal documentation, merchandise selection and emotional support.

sources.title: Service Corporation International 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Reports funeral locations, acquisitions, pre-need backlog, average revenue per service, cremation rate, gross profit and trust-accounting economics.

sources.title: What Goes Into a Funeral Home Valuation?
sources.what_it_supports: States that average-to-larger funeral homes were valued at 6–7× EBITDA, with higher values for prized firms when real estate is included, and identifies normalization considerations.

sources.title: Park Lawn Corporation — Our History
sources.what_it_supports: Documents multiple funeral-home acquisitions in 2025 and 2026, establishing Park Lawn as an active consolidator.

sources.title: Carriage Services 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Reports 155 funeral homes and the 2025 acquisition of eight funeral homes, one cemetery and one cremation-focused business.

sources.title: Foundation Partners Group Launches Next Chapter
sources.what_it_supports: Reports fresh institutional growth capital and a network of more than 250 funeral homes, cremation centers and cemeteries.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.what_it_supports: Reports Census-based evidence that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and documents broad succession-planning gaps.

sources.title: TDAW Makes Writing Obituaries Easy, Preserves Human Element
sources.what_it_supports: Documents AI-assisted obituary drafting inside funeral-home software and the need for human review; supplier-originated evidence.

sources.title: Funeral Home Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.what_it_supports: Provides 2021–2025 small-business sale benchmarks based on seller discretionary earnings and revenue, useful only as a lower-market cross-check rather than an EBITDA comp.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI can reduce administrative, writing, scheduling and routine communication time, but licensed judgment, physical care and family-facing work persist. Fractions are bounded task-share judgments within each supplied role.
caveats: The 4-digit 8122 role mix includes 812220 cemeteries and crematories; groundskeeping is sibling-code contamination for funeral-home-only targets.
Online arrangement and obituary-tool evidence shows digital touchpoints, not representative measurement of labor displacement.

role_judgments.rationale: Visitation, casket movement, processions and family assistance are physical and in-person; only minor coordination automates.
Case documents, permits, obituaries and coordination can be assisted, but arrangements, removals and service direction remain licensed and relational.
Scheduling, compliance, reporting and pre-need administration can compress management time; leadership and community trust remain.
Grounds work is physical and mostly reflects cemetery contamination outside exact 812210.
Routine case records, filings, billing and correspondence are well suited to integrated automation.
Scheduling, document drafting, data entry and routine communications can be heavily automated.
Embalming is licensed physical preparation; documentation and inventory assistance are minor.
Lead follow-up, proposals and pre-need paperwork automate, while trust-based selling and counseling remain human.
Routine calls, appointment intake and information requests can be automated, with sensitive and complex calls escalated.
Crematory operation is physical and safety-sensitive; only records and monitoring assistance automate.
The large unlisted tail mixes drivers and physical support with administrative and marketing roles; only a minority is replaceable.

pi_dist.rationale: Families contract directly with funeral providers, which set itemized prices under the Funeral Rule; there is no dominant third-party payer reclaiming administrative savings.
caveats: Itemization increases consumer choice and price comparability; competitive retention is reflected separately in pi_moat.

pi_moat.rationale: Local reputation, facilities, professional guidance and pre-need relationships support retention, but online search, direct cremation and lower-ticket mix constrain pricing power.
caveats: National consumer and public-company evidence may overstate the moat of an acquired home after a family-owner exit.
Cremation-mix price pressure is reflected here and not double-counted as AI-driven terminal disappearance.

t50_years.rationale: Digital arrangements and AI writing tools establish a base, but effective adoption across case administration is slowed by fragmented operators, sensitive interactions and state-specific compliance.
caveats: No representative NAICS-812210 AI-adoption survey was found; online consumer behavior and product availability are proxies.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Online arrangement is common enough to prove digital readiness and AI obituary tools are available, but neither establishes majority effective AI use across operator workflows.
caveats: Nearly 30% online arrangement completion measures consumer channel behavior, not AI-enabled labor replacement.

historical_analogs.value: Online arrangement portals, electronic case management and template-driven obituary production, now augmented by generative AI with human review.
historical_analogs.rationale: These analogs show administrative digitization while preserving professional guidance and final human accountability.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: From drafting assistance only to integrated case-administration workflows
caveats: Digital arrangements are broader than AI, and supplier evidence may overstate deployment.

selection.rationale: A downward adjustment from the 52.3% share of employer businesses owned by people age 55 or older approximates the narrower 60-plus threshold.
caveats: Cross-industry U.S. employer-business proxy; it is not funeral-home-specific and reports age 55+, not 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Broad U.S. succession-planning gaps are documented, but no funeral-home-owner-specific shortage measure was verified.
caveats: Industry workforce constraints and family-business succession anecdotes are not treated as proof of an owner-succession shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: A conservative floor counts four separately verified scaled buyers or institutionally backed consolidators active in 2025–2026: SCI, Park Lawn, Carriage and Foundation Partners.
caveats: This is a verified lower bound and excludes smaller regional groups, family offices and buyers without public disclosures.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected at the midpoint of Johnson Consulting's 6–7× EBITDA range for average-to-larger funeral homes; small-business SDE comps serve only as a lower-market reasonableness check.
caveats: The primary multiple evidence dates from 2022 and says prized firms may be higher when real estate is included.
BizBuySell reports seller-discretionary-earnings multiples for much smaller deals and is not directly comparable to EBITDA.
Normalize owner labor, market rent, vehicles, pre-need trust earnings and deferred obligations before applying the multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: A diversified, professionally managed portfolio is assigned the top of the same cited 6–7× EBITDA range, only 0.5× above the midpoint entry assumption.
caveats: The exit premium requires reduced owner dependence and durable local volume; cremation mix or loss of the selling family's goodwill can erase it.
Real-estate inclusion must be consistent between entry and exit.

pricing_structure.value: Direct-to-consumer itemized at-need and pre-need pricing under the FTC Funeral Rule, with trust- or insurance-funded future obligations and secular mix shift toward cremation.
pricing_structure.rationale: Regulation requires itemization, while industry and public-company evidence show material pre-need economics and a rising cremation mix.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: From higher-ticket burial ceremonies to lower-ticket direct cremation, with local variation in pre-need penetration
caveats: SCI combines funeral, cemetery and cremation activities and is a scaled-company proxy; exact 812210 targets may not own cemeteries or crematories.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The labor opportunity is concentrated in office work and paperwork within licensed roles; the five-year adoption estimate does not imply replacement of physical or compassionate service.

terminal_value.justification: Paid disposition and professional guidance survive AI, but rising cremation and direct-disposition mix reduce revenue per case and can bypass traditional facilities.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Exact 812210 scope is anchored to Census [S1]. The 4-digit OEWS role mix, generic owner-age evidence and scaled public-company economics are disclosed proxies rather than silently treated as exact.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Cremation and direct-disposition mix, itemized price comparison and digital shopping pressure realized revenue; these mechanisms constrain capture and terminal revenue without being misclassified as AI labor replacement.

dataset_mismatch.description: The occupational mix is 4-digit NAICS 8122 and includes 812220 cemeteries and crematories, especially groundskeepers; the exact target industry is 812210 funeral homes and funeral services.
sensitivity: Removing cemetery grounds labor has little effect because its assigned AI fraction is near zero, but it raises the relative weight of funeral-home roles.
The dataset n_band of 40 uses a generic 15.65% margin and likely understates qualifying funeral homes if healthy operators earn higher normalized margins.

subsegments: funeral home and cemetery combination operators
market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Multi-location funeral-home operators with $1–10M normalized EBITDA
market_boundary.selection_basis: Exact 812210 excludes stand-alone cemeteries and crematories, but combination operators and public-company disclosures cross that boundary; score inputs disclose that proxy and focus on funeral-home economics.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The adoption factor ranges from 1.25 to 3.33 and materially changes the composite score.
Administrative task depth and the cemetery-contaminated role proxy can shift available labor value substantially.
An exit at or below entry removes the spread factor; 7× requires portfolio quality despite cremation and owner-transition risks.

reviewer_note: Verify that the transaction multiple is applied consistently with real estate and normalized pre-need accounting; terminal pressure is driven by mix, not AI elimination.
